problem_statement: A legal-document OCR service began returning pages in the wrong order on one processor architecture after a routine worker rebuild. Source revision, service settings, and document bytes appear unchanged, but the rebuild refreshed platform-specific packages and moved jobs onto new hosts. Determine whether the primary cause lies in application logic, dependency resolution, document data, persisted job state, or infrastructure.

observations.statement: Document doc-88 produces page order 1,2,4,3 on arm64 workers and 1,2,3,4 on x86_64 workers under source revision ocr-52 and the same normalized PDF hash.
The same revision and document on arm64 produce page order 1,2,4,3; the resolved package fingerprint differs from x86_64 even though the top-level requirements file is identical.

The arm64 resolution report selects pagegeom 3.8.0, while x86_64 retains 3.7.4 because only 3.8.0 has a wheel for the rebuilt arm image. pagegeom is transitive and absent from the service's direct lock constraints.
Pinning only pagegeom to 3.7.4 on arm64 restores correct order for the 40-document regression corpus; installing 3.8.0 on x86_64 reproduces the same swaps with source, settings, and PDFs fixed.
A bounded call trace shows the service emits page indices in the order returned by pagegeom and performs no architecture-specific sorting branch of its own.
CPU throttling, memory pressure, disk errors, and worker restarts are absent on affected hosts; moving the unmodified 3.8.0 environment to a quiet arm64 host preserves the misordering.
The normalized input PDF has a stable hash across all runs, and an independent PDF inspector reports a conventional four-page object tree with monotonically ordered page references.
Retrying with a new job id and an empty OCR cache returns the same order for a given resolved package set; no prior page-order state is read by the worker.
